title: Probabilistic size-and-shape functional mixed models

reviews.summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.
reviews.strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.
reviews.weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.
reviews.questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.
reviews.limitations: The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This work addresses the problem of estimating functional data—specifically, time series expressed as smooth functions—within a particular observational framework. The authors decompose the observed data into a fixed effect component (represented by a mean function) and variable components (characterized as noise functions), with a norm-preserving temporal warping function serving as a confounding factor. The paper proposes a Bayesian methodology for parameter estimation, incorporating specified distributional assumptions for noise and utilizing basis function representations to work in finite-dimensional spaces. The proposed approach is validated through both synthetic experiments and applications to real functional datasets.

## Strengths

The manuscript provides an effective exposition of various temporal warping models with clear intuitive explanations supported by concrete examples. Particularly valuable is the detailed mathematical and graphical treatment distinguishing norm-preserving warps from value-preserving alternatives, which substantially clarifies these concepts. The work makes a meaningful contribution to functional data analysis, a field with growing relevance across diverse application domains.

The authors strengthen their motivation by drawing connections to historical precedent in 2D shape analysis, offering useful context for understanding the proposed mixed effects framework.

Additionally, the paper demonstrates sound computational judgment in addressing the challenge of infinite-dimensional estimation through projection onto basis functions, with B-splines serving as an effective choice for practical implementation.

## Weaknesses

The experimental validation relies exclusively on comparisons with a single publicly available method (warpMiX), employing the software's default parameter settings without adjustment. Notably, no effort is made to align the noise variance specifications across the two approaches. The default noise variance in warpMiX operates at 10^-3, while the proposed method employs substantially larger values (σ_c² = 0.25, σ² = 1, etc.). The comparability of these results remains unclear, and the review should address how such disparities might affect fair comparison or whether alternative parameterization strategies would yield more meaningful benchmarking.

The paper cites numerous prior works employing comparable models, particularly regarding norm-preserving transformation actions. Relevant literature by Kurtek, Srivastava, and collaborators—some of which appear in the reference list—develops similar techniques for mean function estimation from functional data stacks. The manuscript would benefit substantially from explicit comparative analysis against these existing approaches rather than relying solely on warpMiX.

Regarding evaluation beyond simulation studies, the paper lacks assessment of downstream utility. The recovery of functions and functional parameters should be evaluated through practical application to concrete tasks rather than relying on subjective visual interpretation. For example, the claims in Figure 4 regarding superior estimates that capture "sharp features representative of observed data" (page 9, line 351) constitute subjective judgments without demonstrated quantifiable benefit. The work could be substantially strengthened by evaluating whether parameter recovery leads to measurable improvements in downstream applications—such as classification tasks—thereby providing objective evidence of the method's utility.

## Questions for Authors

How feasible would the methodology be if implemented without basis function approximations, instead representing functions directly through sample values? What aspects of the approach would simplify or become more complex under such a strategy? What practical consequences follow from committing to a particular basis family such as B-splines? The appendix explores data-driven functional PCA bases, which appeared to underperform relative to B-splines; clarification on this trade-off would be helpful.

Additional clarification regarding the experimental design and evaluation strategy is requested, particularly regarding the points raised in the evaluation section above.

## Limitations

The authors provide adequate discussion of model limitations and underlying assumptions, appropriately addressed in Section 3.

## Overall Assessment

**Rating: 7**

The paper makes solid contributions to functional data analysis with clear exposition and reasonable methodology, but suffers from incomplete experimental validation and limited comparative analysis against related work. Addressing the comparison fairness issues, incorporating evaluations on downstream tasks, and providing more comprehensive benchmarking against existing methods would substantially strengthen the work.

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.
reviews.strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.
reviews.questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?
reviews.limitations: See above

2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Functional Mixed Models with Phase Variations

## Summary

This manuscript addresses the problem of estimating a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models under conditions where measurement error and phase variations at the object level complicate recovery. The authors focus on identifying the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under specific transformations. The central hypothesis is that a Bayesian functional mixed model framework can reliably recover these size-and-shape features, with the approach validated through both synthetic and empirical experiments.

## Strengths

1. The paper demonstrates strong expository quality throughout. The writing is clear, self-contained, and mathematically precise—qualities that have become increasingly uncommon in contemporary literature. This rigor in presentation is commendable and enhances the paper's accessibility.

2. The methodological choices are thoroughly justified through careful reasoning and empirical validation. The selection of basis functions, prior specifications, and other model components are all supported by both theoretical arguments and computational demonstrations.

## Major Weaknesses

### Significance and Motivation

1. The paper's central contribution—incorporating phase variations into the model via phase-preserving actions (equations 3-4)—raises important questions about practical relevance:
   - What real-world applications genuinely motivate this modeling choice? Are there empirical domains where phase functions demonstrably act on µ in a phase-preserving manner?
   - What substantive advantages and disadvantages distinguish phase-preserving from value-preserving formulations? The authors provide limited discussion of this distinction (lines 142-147), which I address further in the clarity section below.

2. The empirical comparison with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) appears insufficient for drawing meaningful conclusions. Without ground truth, it becomes difficult to assess which method's estimate of µ is more credible. The two approaches yield different results, but the paper offers insufficient guidance on interpreting which is preferable, making the practical value of the proposed method unclear.

### Clarity Issues

1. **Lines 142-147**: The claim that equivalence classes under norm-preserving actions are "larger" than those under value-preserving actions requires clarification:
   - The provided justification is unconvincing. The same argument could be applied to value-preserving actions: for any g and γ(t), one can construct f(t) := g(γ^{-1}(t)) such that f(γ(t)) = g(γ^{-1}(γ(t))) = g(t).
   - Footnote 1 offers only an informal argument. A rigorous proof or precise reference would substantially strengthen this claim.

2. **Introductory structure**: The motivation is somewhat convoluted. The opening uses the Berkeley growth data to justify incorporating constraints due to high dimensionality, yet equation (1) appears quite general without strong prior assumptions. The second paragraph then introduces phase variability, further complicating the model. This layering of motivations obscures the fundamental problem being addressed. A clearer articulation of why model (1) alone is insufficient and why phase variability is necessary would improve the narrative flow.

3. **Section 2 positioning**: While the background on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations is helpful, the section's relevance is unclear before reading further. Explicitly stating in the preamble which properties are essential to the solution and what new capabilities they enable would improve readability and motivation.

4. **Minor notation issue**: Line 119 appears to have a typographical error with γ.

## Novelty and Technical Depth

The underlying idea that non-minimal parameterizations create equivalence classes of solutions is well-established, as the authors acknowledge (equation 2). Drawing on geometric vision literature, I would highlight several important considerations:

1. A standard approach to resolving such ambiguity is enforcing constraints that select preferred elements from each equivalence class—essentially what occurs near line 76. However, a critical distinction exists: while two parameter sets may be equivalent in the noise-free case, they can behave quite differently under noise. Consequently, the choice of which equivalence class element to select is consequential. For instance, Hartley (1997) demonstrates that particular normalization choices improve numerical stability in noisy settings.

2. Alternatively, the ambiguity could be addressed by estimating the entire equivalence class rather than selecting a single representative, as explored in works such as Ding et al. (2020) on robust homography estimation.

While this paper need not exhaust all possibilities, a discussion of how optimal the constraint choice at line 76 is, and what alternative approaches might offer, would strengthen the contribution. Specifically, how does this choice perform under noise or outlier corruption compared to other strategies?

## Minor Comments and Questions

1. How does posterior inference via MCMC compare to direct optimization of the log-likelihood? What are the computational and inferential trade-offs?

2. The introductory narrative could be streamlined. The Berkeley growth example motivates dimension reduction, yet this is somewhat disconnected from the phase variability problem that dominates later sections. Clarifying why both issues matter and how they relate would improve coherence.

3. In Section 2, explicitly state at the outset which properties of phase functions and size-and-shape transformations are critical to solving the problem, and which enable novel capabilities. This would contextualize the background material more effectively.

4. Line 119: Verify notation (γ in Gamma?).

## Overall Assessment

The paper presents competent technical work with clear exposition and careful justification of design choices. However, it suffers from unclear practical motivation and insufficient discussion of why the phase-preserving approach is preferable to alternatives. The empirical validation, particularly on real data, does not definitively establish the method's advantages. The novelty is somewhat incremental given existing work on equivalence class ambiguity, and the paper would benefit from deeper engagement with related literature on parameter selection strategies under noise.

## Rating

**7**

Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7

reviews.text:
# Peer Review (Rewritten)

## Summary

This paper addresses uncertainty quantification in one-dimensional regression problems with noisy observations. The authors propose a sophisticated additive modeling framework that incorporates invariances based on space-time unitary transformations. Comparative experiments on numerical benchmarks show the proposed method outperforming existing state-of-the-art techniques.

## Strengths

- The incorporation of strong structural assumptions enables the method to successfully recover ground-truth signals in high-noise regimes where conventional approaches (e.g., Gaussian processes) struggle significantly on synthetic problems.
- The methodology demonstrates practical utility when applied to real-world data, as evidenced by its performance on the Berkeley growth spurt dataset.

## Weaknesses

- The scope and applicability of this work appear limited to a specialized domain, raising concerns about whether the broader machine learning community will find the contribution sufficiently relevant or impactful.
- Despite a clearly written introduction, the paper presents substantial challenges in comprehension due to its sophisticated mathematical framework and dense notation. After careful reading, the fundamental value proposition—specifically, why practitioners and researchers should prioritize functional mixed models and their Bayesian treatment—remains inadequately motivated.

## Questions and Concerns

The selection of NeurIPS as the target venue seems questionable for this contribution. The work may find greater resonance and recognition in a more specialized forum. Considerable effort was required to parse the technical content, yet the broader significance for the machine learning community and concrete motivations for the proposed approach remain ambiguous. The paper might be better positioned for a statistics-focused journal, particularly given the frequent citations to such venues throughout the manuscript. Should the authors substantially strengthen the general motivation and applications, I would reconsider my assessment; however, accessibility challenges would persist as a concern.

## Limitations

The authors have appropriately addressed all relevant limitations.

## Rating

5

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This manuscript addresses the problem of reliably estimating a fixed effect function μ through posterior sampling and inference in a functional mixed model framework. The model incorporates random phase and amplitude variation at the object level while avoiding restrictive finite-rank assumptions on the error covariance structure.

----
**Post-rebuttal note:** The authors' response has adequately addressed the majority of my initial concerns, and I am revising my assessment upward accordingly.

## Strengths

1) The paper tackles a non-trivial problem: recovering both the magnitude and shape characteristics of an unknown function via sampling-based inference.

2) The theoretical contributions are noteworthy, particularly the careful derivation of the Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio for this complex setting.

3) The authors provide empirical validation of convergence properties through carefully designed numerical experiments.

## Weaknesses

1) The proposed approach appears to require specification of distinct prior models depending on the particular function being estimated. This raises questions about the generalizability and practical applicability of the methodology.

2) Critical information is missing regarding the sensitivity of the method to the hyperparameter θ and the computational costs associated with the proposed algorithm. These practical considerations warrant explicit treatment.

## Questions and Concerns

1) Neural network-based approaches can also perform function recovery through sampling mechanisms. The manuscript would benefit from a clear articulation of how the proposed method compares favorably to such alternatives in terms of efficiency, interpretability, or other relevant dimensions.

2) The model specified in Equation (3) appears to be fixed. How can this single model specification accommodate the recovery of functions with substantially different characteristics?

## Overall Assessment

**Rating: 5**

This work presents a solid contribution with interesting theoretical development and reasonable empirical validation. The post-rebuttal revisions have resolved most substantive concerns, supporting acceptance of the manuscript.

reviews.summary: In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.
reviews.strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.
reviews.weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.
reviews.questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?
reviews.limitations: The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
# Peer Review - Rewritten

## Summary

This work introduces a Bayesian mixed-effects model formulated within a functional Hilbert space framework to analyze size-and-shape characteristics of square-integrable fixed effects. The methodology leverages an isometric action of an infinite-dimensional phase function group. The authors validate their approach through both simulated and empirical datasets, comparing performance against existing alternatives and demonstrating that the posterior mean effectively captures essential functional properties under their proposed model.

## Strengths

+ The paper presents a novel Bayesian functional mixed-effects framework that accommodates phase variation without restrictive constraints.

+ The introduction of a size-and-shape functional perspective offers a fresh geometric interpretation for functional data analysis.

+ The manuscript is generally well-organized and readable, with adequate explanations and discussions throughout most sections. The presentation flows logically, though some technical formulations require careful attention.

## Weaknesses

- The notation system requires refinement. Specifically, distinguishing between vectors, matrices, and scalars through consistent typographic conventions would enhance clarity. Including explicit dimensionality statements in key passages would further benefit readers. Additionally, certain symbols (notably the transpose operator) are used inconsistently throughout the manuscript.

- The approach relies on linear combinations of basis functions with predetermined subspace ranks (Bf and Br). This formulation closely parallels existing methodologies in the literature, particularly LDA-inspired approaches, where subspace dimensionality is fixed a priori.

- The manuscript lacks concrete practical motivation. The authors would benefit from articulating specific application domains where their method is needed and explaining why existing alternatives are insufficient for these contexts.

## Questions and Concerns

How are the subspace ranks Bf and Br determined in practice—manually or through some systematic procedure? The sensitivity of results to these hyperparameter choices requires investigation. Could these ranks be estimated automatically from the data rather than fixed beforehand? This appears to be a critical modeling choice that deserves thorough discussion.

Why specifically employ B-spline bases as the predefined basis functions? The literature offers numerous alternative piecewise polynomial representations that might warrant comparison or justification.

Is it feasible to learn basis functions jointly with their corresponding weight coefficients, rather than using fixed basis systems?

The sample size discrepancy between real (n=93) and synthetic (n=100,000) experiments is puzzling. If the research aims to address challenges in data-limited scenarios, the synthetic experiments should reflect comparable sample sizes rather than employing drastically larger datasets.

Figures 1(b) and 1(c) lack citation in the introductory section.

While the consistent superiority over warpMix on synthetic benchmarks is encouraging, the evaluation would be strengthened by including additional datasets. Furthermore, the computational efficiency comparison is absent—presenting accuracy-versus-computational-cost trade-offs for both methods would provide important practical insights.

Have the authors examined failure modes or boundary cases where the method performs poorly?

## Limitations

The paper does not adequately discuss its limitations. That said, the work does not raise apparent concerns regarding negative societal impacts.

## Overall Assessment

**Rating: 5**

title: Chain of Preference Optimization: Improving Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in LLMs

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO) to improve reasoning in LLMs. CPO utilizes preference data generated during ToT to fine-tune models. This approach improves reasoning without increasing inference complexity.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of CPO is interesting.
2. CPO achieves enhanced performance without increasing inference complexity.
3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. While CPO is effective across several tasks, its performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark, remains unexplored.
2. Fine-tuning with reasoning paths generated by ToT may reduce the diversity of generated paths. A systematic investigation into how the generated reasoning paths change before and after fine-tuning is needed.
3. The effectiveness of CPO is heavily dependent on the quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. If ToT produces suboptimal paths, the preference data might not be as beneficial.
4. The paper does not adequately address the applicability of CPO to more advanced models, such as LLaMA3.
5. While CPO enhances performance, the paper lacks a deep analysis of the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. Understanding why certain paths are preferred could provide additional insights and improve trust in the model’s decisions.
reviews.limitations: Yes

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary
This paper proposes Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO), a method designed to enhance reasoning capabilities in large language models by leveraging preference data derived from Tree-of-Thought (ToT) trajectories for model fine-tuning. A key advantage of the approach is that it achieves performance improvements without imposing additional computational burden during inference.

## Strengths
1. The core concept underlying CPO presents a novel and compelling contribution to the field.
2. The method demonstrates measurable performance gains while maintaining inference efficiency, which is a practical advantage for deployment.
3. The manuscript is clearly articulated and presents its ideas in an accessible manner that facilitates understanding.

## Weaknesses
1. The evaluation scope is limited with respect to challenging reasoning tasks—specifically, the paper does not provide results on demanding benchmarks such as MATH, leaving questions about CPO's effectiveness on more difficult problems unanswered.
2. There is insufficient analysis of how fine-tuning on ToT-generated reasoning paths affects the diversity of outputs. A thorough comparative study documenting how the characteristics of generated reasoning paths evolve through the fine-tuning process would strengthen the work.
3. The approach's success is contingent upon the quality of reasoning trajectories produced by ToT. The paper does not adequately discuss the implications when ToT generates suboptimal or flawed paths, and how this degradation propagates to the preference data used for optimization.
4. The generalizability of CPO to state-of-the-art models remains unclear. The paper lacks discussion of whether CPO remains effective when applied to more recent and capable architectures, such as LLaMA3.
5. While CPO delivers performance improvements, the paper provides limited investigation into the interpretability of the resulting reasoning paths. A deeper examination of what makes certain paths preferable and why the model learns these preferences would enhance transparency and confidence in the approach.

## Limitations
Acknowledged

## Rating
6

reviews.summary: The paper presents a novel method to enhance the performance of LLMs in complex problem-solving tasks using Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO). The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The CPO method aligns each step of the CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT, leveraging the inherent preference information in the tree-search process. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.
reviews.strengths: The paper presents a novel method, Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), that significantly enhances the CoT reasoning ability of LLMs without increasing the inference load. It offers a convincing comparison with the ToT method, demonstrating that CPO substantially reduces inference time, as evidenced by the decrease in latency from 1749s/ins to 38s/ins on Llama2-7B. Furthermore, the paper shows that CPO not only matches but surpasses the ToT baseline in terms of accuracy across multiple datasets. This indicates that CPO effectively fine-tunes LLMs to generate more optimal reasoning paths, thereby improving their performance in complex problem-solving tasks. The research is significant as it provides a practical solution to the challenge of balancing inference complexity and reasoning quality in LLMs.
reviews.weaknesses: The paper presents a promising approach to fine-tuning LLMs using CPO. However, there is a significant gap in the evaluation of the potential impacts of this fine-tuning process on other abilities of the pretrained LLMs. This omission could leave readers questioning the broader implications and versatility of the proposed method. Future work should consider a comprehensive evaluation of the fine-tuning process to fully understand its effects on the LLMs' overall performance.
reviews.questions:
LN35, what is the advantage of CPO comared to existing MCTS methods? And why?

LN117, have you considered online RL that regenerates the chain-of-preferences using the updated LLM?

Table1, I'm wondering how does the method perform on common reasoning tasks like GSM8K and ProofWriter?
reviews.limitations: Yes.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents a novel method to enhance the performance of LLMs in complex problem-solving tasks using Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO). The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The CPO method aligns each step of the CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT, leveraging the inherent preference information in the tree-search process. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.

Strengths:
The paper presents a novel method, Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), that significantly enhances the CoT reasoning ability of LLMs without increasing the inference load. It offers a convincing comparison with the ToT method, demonstrating that CPO substantially reduces inference time, as evidenced by the decrease in latency from 1749s/ins to 38s/ins on Llama2-7B. Furthermore, the paper shows that CPO not only matches but surpasses the ToT baseline in terms of accuracy across multiple datasets. This indicates that CPO effectively fine-tunes LLMs to generate more optimal reasoning paths, thereby improving their performance in complex problem-solving tasks. The research is significant as it provides a practical solution to the challenge of balancing inference complexity and reasoning quality in LLMs.

Weaknesses:
The paper presents a promising approach to fine-tuning LLMs using CPO. However, there is a significant gap in the evaluation of the potential impacts of this fine-tuning process on other abilities of the pretrained LLMs. This omission could leave readers questioning the broader implications and versatility of the proposed method. Future work should consider a comprehensive evaluation of the fine-tuning process to fully understand its effects on the LLMs' overall performance.

Questions:
LN35, what is the advantage of CPO comared to existing MCTS methods? And why?

LN117, have you considered online RL that regenerates the chain-of-preferences using the updated LLM?

Table1, I'm wondering how does the method perform on common reasoning tasks like GSM8K and ProofWriter?

Limitations:
Yes.

Rating:
6

# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This work introduces Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), a technique designed to improve the reasoning capabilities of large language models on challenging problem-solving tasks. The core contribution involves leveraging tree-of-thought (ToT) search trees to guide the fine-tuning of language models, enabling them to produce reasoning chains comparable to or exceeding ToT performance while avoiding the computational overhead of tree search at inference time. The approach works by aligning each intermediate step in CoT reasoning trajectories with corresponding steps identified through ToT exploration, thereby extracting and incorporating preference signals embedded in the tree-search process. The authors demonstrate the method's utility across multiple benchmarks including question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning tasks.

## Strengths

The paper contributes a novel and practical approach through CPO that meaningfully improves the chain-of-thought reasoning performance of LLMs without imposing additional computational burden during inference. A particularly compelling aspect is the direct comparison with ToT, which demonstrates that CPO achieves substantial efficiency gains—reducing latency from 1749s/ins to 38s/ins on Llama2-7B—while simultaneously maintaining or even exceeding the accuracy performance of the more computationally expensive ToT baseline across multiple datasets. This dual achievement suggests that CPO effectively guides models to discover and generate superior reasoning paths, thereby enhancing their problem-solving capabilities. The work addresses a genuine challenge in the field: how to leverage the benefits of intensive search-based reasoning during training while maintaining practical inference efficiency at deployment time.

## Weaknesses

While the paper presents a compelling approach to enhancing LLM reasoning through CPO-guided fine-tuning, a notable limitation is the insufficient examination of how this fine-tuning procedure affects the broader capability landscape of the underlying pretrained models. The lack of such analysis raises important questions about potential side effects and the generalizability of the approach across diverse model functionalities. To fully establish the robustness and applicability of this method, subsequent research should conduct a thorough investigation into how the fine-tuning process influences the overall performance profile of the LLMs.

## Questions

- Regarding line 35: How does CPO compare to existing Monte Carlo tree search methods in terms of advantages, and what is the underlying rationale for these differences?

- Regarding line 117: Have the authors explored the possibility of incorporating online reinforcement learning approaches that would enable continuous regeneration of preference chains using the progressively updated language model?

- Concerning Table 1: What is the empirical performance of the proposed method when evaluated on standard reasoning benchmarks such as GSM8K and ProofWriter?

## Limitations

The authors acknowledge limitations in their work.

## Overall Assessment

**Rating: 6**

reviews.summary:
This paper presents Chain-of-preference optimization (CPO) a self-supervised learning extension of Tree of Thought (ToT). Rather than use ToT during test time, which takes exponentially longer than end-to-end sampling, this paper proposes to use ToT at training time to annotate data for DPO fine-tuning and then use the DPO-tuned model end-to-end at test time. Notably, the DPO annotations are performed by the untuned model itself being prompted to label inferences as either useful or not. This differs from other approaches that are trained on only full successful reasoning paths.

For certain QA and reasoning tasks, the paper presents experimental evidence that CPO yields stronger models than either (1) fine-tuning the same LM on positive examples (TS-SFT) as done in previous papers or (2) running regular chain-of-thought prompting.
reviews.strengths:
1. The approach is an intuitive and straightforward improvement to multi-hop reasoning that that is much faster than tree-based inference procedures.
2. The approach is appealing as it doesn't rely on correct labels nor external LLMs other than the LLM being fine-tuned on its own preference data.
3. The methodology is well explained and easily reproducible.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. While the authors run evaluation over several datasets, they only consider 300 questions per dataset which is quite small. A stronger evaluation would use the whole test sets.
2. Details about the baseline implementation are unclear. The authors refer to both "TS-SFT" and "TS-LLM" (l.206) which in the original paper refer to different things-- SFT refers to the model fine-tuned on training examples with reasoning traces pulled from both a gold-annotated dataset and/or reasoning traces from the model that led to correct answers. However, TS-LLM in the original paper refers to the result of an iterative refinement process.
3. The choice of datasets is somewhat odd. E.g. HotpotQA is meant to be evaluated against support documents, which might explain the very low scores. They also compare against approaches (ToT, TS-SFT) that were not evaluated on any of the QA or Fact Verification tasks under consideration.  This paper did _not_ consider the Game of 24 or GSM datasets, which both ToT and TS-SFT did evaluate. 

Missing references:
1. Khalifa et al 2023: [GRACE: Discriminator-Guided Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://aclanthology.org/2023.findings-emnlp.1022.pdf)
2. Li et al 2023: [Learning Math Reasoning from Self-Sampled Correct and Partially-Correct Solutions](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=4D4TSJE6-K)
reviews.questions:
* What metrics did you use for the QA datasets? many of them use more than exact match, e.g. HotpotQA uses F1.
* The discussion around ablations in 5.3 is unclear. Are the trends consistent across different models and datasets? 
* It is odd to have only considered up to 200 instances for constructing preference pairs. Why is this experiment limited to such a small maximum? The difference between e.g. 160 and 200 seems much less important than the difference between 200 and 1000 or 1000 and 5000. 
* How many total preference pairs do you end up training on? It would be helpful to include this number in the main body of the paper.
* How stable is this approach to different prompts for state evaluation? Did you experiment with other prompts?
reviews.limitations: yes

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Chain-of-Preference Optimization

## Summary

This work introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO), which extends Tree of Thought (ToT) methodology by shifting its application from inference time to training time. Rather than incurring the exponential computational cost of ToT during deployment, the authors propose leveraging ToT during training to generate preference annotations for subsequent DPO fine-tuning, enabling efficient end-to-end inference at test time. A distinctive feature of this approach is that preference labels are generated through self-annotation by the base model itself, which classifies its own reasoning steps as helpful or unhelpful, departing from prior work that relies exclusively on fully correct reasoning trajectories.

The paper demonstrates empirical improvements on select question-answering and reasoning benchmarks, showing that CPO outperforms both supervised fine-tuning baselines trained on successful trajectories (TS-SFT) and standard chain-of-thought prompting approaches.

## Strengths

1. The proposed method represents a pragmatic and elegant refinement to multi-step reasoning that achieves substantially better computational efficiency compared to tree-search inference strategies.

2. The approach is particularly attractive in its independence from gold-standard annotations and its avoidance of reliance on external language models—instead, the model learns from preference signals derived from its own outputs.

3. The technical presentation is clear and transparent, with sufficient detail to facilitate reproduction of the results.

## Weaknesses

1. The evaluation scope is limited in scale. The authors restrict their assessment to merely 300 instances per dataset, which represents a narrow sample. A more convincing evaluation would incorporate the complete test sets to ensure robustness and statistical significance of the reported improvements.

2. The baseline implementations lack sufficient clarity. The paper conflates "TS-SFT" and "TS-LLM" (line 206), which denote distinct methodologies in the original source material—TS-SFT designates a model fine-tuned on reasoning traces sourced from gold annotations and/or model-generated trajectories leading to correct answers, whereas TS-LLM describes an iterative refinement procedure. This conflation creates ambiguity about which baseline is actually being compared against.

3. The dataset selection raises concerns about experimental design validity. HotpotQA, for instance, is designed for evaluation against supporting documents, which may account for the notably poor performance observed. Furthermore, the paper does not include comparisons on benchmarks where the baseline methods (ToT, TS-SFT) were originally evaluated—specifically, the Game of 24 and GSM datasets are absent from this work. Conversely, the QA and fact verification tasks studied here were not evaluated in the original baseline papers, making direct comparison problematic.

## Missing References

1. Khalifa et al. (2023): [GRACE: Discriminator-Guided Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://aclanthology.org/2023.findings-emnlp.1022.pdf)

2. Li et al. (2023): [Learning Math Reasoning from Self-Sampled Correct and Partially-Correct Solutions](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=4D4TSJE6-K)

## Clarifying Questions

* Which evaluation metrics were employed for the QA datasets? Many such benchmarks employ multiple metrics beyond exact match—for example, HotpotQA incorporates F1 scoring.

* Section 5.3's ablation study presentation is ambiguous. Do the observed patterns generalize consistently across different model sizes and dataset types?

* The preference pair construction experiment is constrained to a maximum of 200 instances, which appears arbitrary. What motivated this ceiling? Investigating the progression from 200 to 1000 or beyond would be considerably more informative than the narrow 160-200 range examined.

* What is the total volume of preference pairs used during training? Reporting this figure in the main text would strengthen reproducibility and aid interpretation of the results.

* How sensitive is the approach to variations in the prompt used for state evaluation? Have alternative prompting strategies been tested to assess robustness?

## Limitations

Adequately addressed.

## Recommendation

**Rating: 6**

The paper presents a reasonable contribution with practical merit and clear presentation. However, significant limitations in experimental scope, baseline clarity, and dataset selection choices prevent a stronger endorsement. The restricted evaluation size, conflation of baseline methodologies, and misalignment between evaluated and original benchmark datasets substantially weaken the empirical claims. Addressing these concerns—particularly through more comprehensive evaluation on standard benchmarks and clarification of baseline implementations—would substantially strengthen the work.

reviews.text:
# Rewritten Peer Review

## Summary

This manuscript introduces Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), a technique that leverages tree-of-thought (ToT) methodology to enhance chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning in large language models during fine-tuning. The core contribution involves aligning intermediate reasoning steps of CoT trajectories with those generated through ToT exploration by extracting preference signals from the tree-search procedure. The proposed approach achieves comparable or superior results relative to standard CoT while eliminating the computational overhead associated with ToT inference. The empirical evaluation supports the method's viability.

## Strengths

**Methodological contribution**: The approach of generating preference datasets through ToT exploration represents a reasonably novel contribution to the field.

**Presentation quality**: The manuscript demonstrates strong writing quality throughout, featuring clear articulation of concepts, precise terminology, and logical coherence. The organizational structure is well-designed, facilitating reader comprehension and maintaining engagement.

**Comprehensive experimental evaluation**: The authors have conducted thorough empirical validation across multiple experimental settings.

## Weaknesses

1. **Insufficient baseline comparisons**: The proposed method falls within the category of self-improvement techniques for language models, yet the experimental comparison lacks engagement with established self-improvement baselines. The related work section references approaches such as ReST and self-rewarding models, but these critical methods do not appear in the comparative evaluation. Including these established self-improvement techniques would substantially strengthen the empirical claims.

2. **Computational efficiency concerns**: The ToT-based dataset construction process introduces significant computational demands that warrant explicit discussion regarding scalability and practical feasibility.

3. **Absence of task-specific prompt engineering details**: While the authors mention general guideline prompts (line 141), the paper does not provide explicit task-specific prompt templates used during preference dataset construction. Although not strictly mandatory, the quality and characteristics of preference datasets are substantially influenced by prompt design choices, and transparency regarding these design decisions would enhance reproducibility and allow assessment of potential biases.

## Questions for Authors

1. What justification supports the selection of DPO over alternative preference-based optimization algorithms?

2. Given the paper's positioning within self-improvement literature, why were comparable self-improvement baseline methods excluded from the experimental comparison?

3. The ToT-based dataset construction may produce substantial quantities of negative samples, potentially resulting in class imbalance within the preference dataset. Does this distributional skew introduce training difficulties or degrade optimization performance?

## Limitations Discussion

The authors have appropriately addressed both the methodological limitations of their work and potential societal implications, demonstrating responsible scholarship in this regard.

## Overall Assessment

**Rating: 6**

reviews.summary: This paper introduces Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), a method to enhance mathematical reasoning in large language models by feeding step-level pairs rather than response-level ones into DPO objectives. CPO leverages non-optimal reasoning thoughts from tree-search processes to construct paired preference data at each reasoning step, where both responses share the same content until a certain step and then start varying. Experiments across seven datasets show CPO significantly improves performance compared to Chain-of-Thought approaches, achieving comparable results to Tree-of-Thought methods while being substantially faster during inference. The authors provide a comprehensive analysis of CPO's components and demonstrate its effectiveness in utilizing both preferred and dispreferred thoughts in the reasoning process.
reviews.strengths:
- The experiments demonstrate that CPO is effective across different tasks including question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic. 
- This paper conducts an extensive analysis of several factors that can influence the model performance. The insights on scaling effects, data mixture, and dispreferred information can be useful.
- The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The major concern is that despite being effective, the method is basically a new use of the DPO algorithm with different inputs (converting response-level pairs to chain-level) and thus it's fundamentally still DPO.
- In section 6, the authors have explained why chain-level optimization is important. However, according to the explanation, token-level DPO is more natural in locating errors and avoiding LCP gradient cancellation. Then according to your theory, why not directly use token-level optimization, which is exactly what PPO does, but an intermediate chain-level? I suppose there exists an efficiency-effectiveness trade-off in these settings.
reviews.questions: See weakness.
reviews.limitations: See above
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), a method to enhance mathematical reasoning in large language models by feeding step-level pairs rather than response-level ones into DPO objectives. CPO leverages non-optimal reasoning thoughts from tree-search processes to construct paired preference data at each reasoning step, where both responses share the same content until a certain step and then start varying. Experiments across seven datasets show CPO significantly improves performance compared to Chain-of-Thought approaches, achieving comparable results to Tree-of-Thought methods while being substantially faster during inference. The authors provide a comprehensive analysis of CPO's components and demonstrate its effectiveness in utilizing both preferred and dispreferred thoughts in the reasoning process.

Strengths:
- The experiments demonstrate that CPO is effective across different tasks including question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic. 
- This paper conducts an extensive analysis of several factors that can influence the model performance. The insights on scaling effects, data mixture, and dispreferred information can be useful.
- The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

Weaknesses:
- The major concern is that despite being effective, the method is basically a new use of the DPO algorithm with different inputs (converting response-level pairs to chain-level) and thus it's fundamentally still DPO.
- In section 6, the authors have explained why chain-level optimization is important. However, according to the explanation, token-level DPO is more natural in locating errors and avoiding LCP gradient cancellation. Then according to your theory, why not directly use token-level optimization, which is exactly what PPO does, but an intermediate chain-level? I suppose there exists an efficiency-effectiveness trade-off in these settings.

Questions:
See weakness.

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
Summary:
This work presents Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), a technique designed to strengthen mathematical reasoning capabilities in large language models by operating on step-level preference pairs rather than full-response pairs within a DPO framework. The approach utilizes suboptimal reasoning trajectories obtained through tree-search to generate preference data at intermediate reasoning steps, where paired outputs maintain identical content up to a divergence point. Evaluation across seven benchmarks demonstrates that CPO achieves notable improvements over standard Chain-of-Thought baselines and matches the performance of Tree-of-Thought methods while offering significant computational efficiency gains at inference time. The paper includes thorough ablation studies examining CPO's design choices and illustrates how the method effectively leverages both preferred and dispreferred reasoning paths.

Strengths:
- CPO demonstrates strong empirical performance across diverse problem domains, including question answering, fact verification, and mathematical computation tasks.
- The paper provides a thorough empirical investigation into multiple design dimensions affecting model performance, yielding valuable findings regarding scaling behavior, dataset composition effects, and the role of dispreferred signals in optimization.
- The manuscript is clearly written with logical organization that facilitates understanding of the method and results.

Weaknesses:
- The primary limitation is that despite its empirical success, the core contribution amounts to a modified application of the existing DPO algorithm with reformatted inputs (restructuring response-level pairs into step-level pairs). Fundamentally, this remains an instantiation of DPO rather than a novel algorithmic framework.
- In Section 6, the authors justify why step-level optimization provides advantages over response-level approaches. Notably, their own reasoning indicates that token-level DPO would be theoretically superior for error localization and avoiding LCP gradient cancellation issues. This raises a critical question: given that token-level optimization addresses these concerns more directly—and that PPO essentially implements token-level updates—what is the principled justification for the intermediate step-level formulation? The authors appear to be navigating an efficiency-effectiveness trade-off, but this is not explicitly articulated or thoroughly analyzed.

Questions:
See weaknesses section above.

Limitations:
See weaknesses section above.

Rating:
5

**Decision: The same acceptance/rejection recommendation as the original review**
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), a method to enhance mathematical reasoning in large language models by feeding step-level pairs rather than response-level ones into DPO objectives. CPO leverages non-optimal reasoning thoughts from tree-search processes to construct paired preference data at each reasoning step, where both responses share the same content until a certain step and then start varying. Experiments across seven datasets show CPO significantly improves performance compared to Chain-of-Thought approaches, achieving comparable results to Tree-of-Thought methods while being substantially faster during inference. The authors provide a comprehensive analysis of CPO's components and demonstrate its effectiveness in utilizing both preferred and dispreferred thoughts in the reasoning process.

Strengths:
- The experiments demonstrate that CPO is effective across different tasks including question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic. 
- This paper conducts an extensive analysis of several factors that can influence the model performance. The insights on scaling effects, data mixture, and dispreferred information can be useful.
- The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

Weaknesses:
- The major concern is that despite being effective, the method is basically a new use of the DPO algorithm with different inputs (converting response-level pairs to chain-level) and thus it's fundamentally still DPO.
- In section 6, the authors have explained why chain-level optimization is important. However, according to the explanation, token-level DPO is more natural in locating errors and avoiding LCP gradient cancellation. Then according to your theory, why not directly use token-level optimization, which is exactly what PPO does, but an intermediate chain-level? I suppose there exists an efficiency-effectiveness trade-off in these settings.

Questions:
See weakness.

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
5

title: Sourcerer: Sample-based Maximum Entropy Source Distribution Estimation

reviews.summary:
This paper deals with the problem of identifying the distribution of a source variable $s$ that generates observations $x$.

## The problem

The authors propose to minimize a classical objective consisting of two terms: 

1. A reconstruction term, that encourages the recovered source distribution to induce a distribution over observations that is close to the data distribution, in a certain divergence (here, the sliced Wasserstein)

2. A regularization term, that encourages the recovered source distribution to be close to a reference distribution, in a certain divergence (here, the reverse KL). 

Many different source distributions can minimize the first term alone, which motivates the second term to contrain the solution space. 

## The probability model

The probability model $p(x)$ can be expressed in terms of two other model distributions: the source distribution $p(s)$ and the likelihood $p(x | s)$.  

Term (1) uses a (sliced) Wasserstein divergence. Computing this divergence requires data samples and samples from the model in two steps:  $s \sim p(s)$ then $x | s \sim p(x | s)$. Differentiating through this divergence requires the probability model, and therefore $p(s)$ and $p(x | s)$, to be samplable and the sampling process should be differentiable. 

Term (2) uses a reverse KL divergence. Computing this divergence requires evaluating the densities of the model and reference distributions, as well as samples from the model in two steps:  $s \sim p(s)$ then $x | s \sim p(x | s)$. Differentiating through this divergence requires the probability model, and therefore $p(s)$ and $p(x | s)$, to be samplable and the sampling process should be differentiable. Additionally, the model density should be known or else a differentiable approximation of the divergence (the authors use the Kozachenko-Leonenko estimator) should be available. 

## Numerics

The authors validate their method using synthetic data where the source is known, and real data where the observations are neurophyisiological recordings and the likelihood is given by a mechanistic model.
reviews.strengths: The presentation is clear and well-explained.
reviews.weaknesses:
Overall, I have two concerns. 

1. The computational constraints on the model are heavy

The choices of divergences (Sliced-Wasserstein and reverse KL) in the cost function impose a number of computational constraints of the model.

Term 1 in the cost uses a vanilla Sliced-Wasserstein divergence estimator that is sample-based. This means the likelihood model $p(x | s)$ should be samplable and the sampling process should be differentiable. The authors propose to train a differentiable approximator of a black-box simulator. To be fair, the authors acknowledge that this is a general issue beyond their paper. 

Term 2 in the cost uses a vanilla KL divergence estimator which requires knowing the density of the model, but the authors circumvent this by using another estimator named "Kozachenko-Leonenko". Given that the authors already used a sample-based divergence in Term 1, wouldn't using another sample-based divergence in the Term 2 (e.g. the Sliced-Wasserstein again, or an MMD) between the model and reference distributions not add any further computational constraints on the model?

2. The evaluation procedure or goal remains unclear to me

The framework of minimizing a cost made up of a reconstruction and regularization term is well-studied in optimization. Once could imagine two natural evaluation procedures:
- how well the true sources are recovered? But the authors are clear that designing an identifiable probability model where the sources can be recovered (up to acceptable indeterminacies) is not the goal here. 
- how efficient is their method on a new test dataset, as compared to other similar methods?

It seems to be that the authors answer the latter, but it is not clear to me what are the other methods to benchmark against.
reviews.questions: Could the authors address the weaknesses part?
reviews.limitations: Yes.

- how efficient is their method on a new test dataset, as compared to other similar methods?

It seems to be that the authors answer the latter, but it is not clear to me what are the other methods to benchmark against.

Questions:

Limitations:
